item.title: Re: bruh
item.content:encoded: Incidentally, those aren't footnotes. Rather, they're an indication for how many names SCP-4853 is assigned, as indicated in the containment procedures ("A numeric identifier for each active reference has been placed next to its first appearance").

item.title: bruh
item.content:encoded: why are there footnote numbers with no footnote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but I'm not sure how I feel about the execution. Also, if there are 10 allowed references, why not flesh it out a bit more? I just feel like it's missing something that would make it more interesting, it just feels like a lackluster antimeme or a reference to the one with the dots. -1, but I really think with either more information, or a little bit of a rewrite, this could be really good.

Edit: I reread it a while after I made this comment and it's good and really doesn't need more, changed to +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pure recursion. Clever, I like it. Take my upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like Inside, but somehow even less concrete. @_@

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Way to call it "this."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Phenomenal. A testament to the power of both the format and of brevity. Just so enjoyable from top to bottom and gets so much done in such a small space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: error[E0502]: cannot borrow `scp_4853` as immutable because it is also borrowed as mutable --> src/scp_index.rs:1012:16 | 312 | display_scp_4853(&mut scp_4853) | ------------- mutable borrow occurs here ... 1012 | .title(&scp_4853); | ----- ^^^^^^^^^ immutable borrow occurs here | | | mutable borrow later used by call

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rust has the same syntax, but references can be immutable as well (&x vs. &mut x).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm unsure about Rust syntax, but in C++ at least, '&' is used to mark something as a 'reference', so I assumed that was the reason for the mainlist name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fabulously clever from beginning to end and I can't believe it took me this long to read it. I don't have a ton of crit to add here, but I did want to say that your experiment sequence at the end is generally a stylistic choice that chides me. Having said that, your interpretation was fresh with each step (more or less) and was important to the narrative, not just used as a stepping stone for another weird effect.

I hope others can reference this as a Scip that 'does it right' when it's time to include their own experiment logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the only important emotion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'what the hell' is a valid emotion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel lukewarm about this.

In my view, the most successful conceptual articles are those that "anchor" their mechanics with a more concrete back story. SCP-4517 is a great example of this, contrasting its "N-ness" with Damien Pisk's feelings of inadequacy. The latter gives us a character to relate to and places the intellectual curiosity of "N" in an emotional context. The result is both clever and impactful.

While this article tries to hint at a broader story with the final note, that aspect feels more like a continuation of the weirdness than a contrast to it. Ultimately, it's exploring abstraction as an end in itself, i.e. weird for the sake of weird. Other people might enjoy that (and looking at the ratings, it seems they do), but it's not something I'm interested in myself. For that reason I'm giving it a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: if anyone is struggling to understand, i've written an explanation on /r/SCPDeclassified (with the help of aismallard, the author) that can be found here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one's got me picking my brain a bit. Kind of hard to comprehend, but then again, thats how most infohazards should be in my opinion. +1 for sure.

Ill be coming back to this to try to glean more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, reminds me a lot of 2719 but has some standout differences. Can't tell if there's more to this than I'm catching right now, or if I'm just overthinking, but I enjoy that feeling. +1.

Also, MacWarren's comment has me curious as well on whether or not that's the intended title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not much to say about this one, just a weird idea executed well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A simple concept executed beautifully. Here, borrow my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title for this article on the mainlist appears to be rather skewed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't entirely get it but I do like the idea of a corporation monopolizing concepts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was doing some programming in Rust when this idea came to me.
